title: Dolmeh (Stuffed Grape Leaves)
description: Tender grape leaves wrapped tightly around a savory filling of ground meat, rice, split peas, and fresh herbs, simmered in a sweet and sour sauce.

ingredients.note: For the sauce (white or apple cider)

instructions.text: If using jarred grape leaves, rinse them very well under cold water to remove the brine. Blanch them in boiling water for 2 minutes to soften them, then drain.
In a skillet, heat 1 tbsp of oil. Sauté the chopped onion until golden. Add the ground meat, turmeric, salt, and pepper. Cook until the meat is browned.
In a large mixing bowl, combine the browned meat, parboiled rice, cooked split peas, and all the chopped herbs (parsley, cilantro, dill, mint). Mix thoroughly. This is your filling.
Lay a grape leaf flat on a cutting board, shiny side down and the stem end facing you. Cut off the stem if still attached.

Place about 1 tablespoon of the filling in the center of the leaf, near the stem end. Fold the bottom flaps over the filling, then fold in the sides, and roll tightly toward the tip of the leaf, like a tiny burrito.
Line the bottom of a wide, heavy pot with a few torn or unused grape leaves to prevent sticking. Arrange the stuffed grape leaves tightly together in the pot, seam side down, in layers.
In a small bowl, mix the pomegranate molasses, sugar, vinegar, 1 tbsp oil, and 1/2 cup of warm water. Pour this sweet and sour sauce over the dolmeh.
Place an inverted heatproof plate on top of the dolmeh inside the pot to keep them submerged and prevent unrolling. Cover the pot with a lid.
Simmer on low heat for 45-50 minutes until the rice is fully cooked, the leaves are tender, and most of the liquid is absorbed.
Let them cool in the pot for 15 minutes before transferring to a serving platter. Serve warm or at room temperature.

tips: Don't overstuff the leaves, as the rice will expand slightly during cooking. Packing them tightly in the pot prevents them from unraveling while simmering.
storage_notes: Dolmeh store incredibly well in the fridge for up to a week. They are excellent served cold directly from the refrigerator.
serve_with: Mast-o-Musir (shallot yogurt), flatbread, and fresh herbs.

substitutions.note: For a vegetarian dolmeh, omit the meat and increase the rice, split peas, or add cooked lentils.
If you prefer a purely sour rather than sweet-and-sour profile, use a mixture of lemon juice, water, and olive oil for the sauce.
